ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for roasted chicken drumstick rice as a composite Singaporean dish; FOODON_00002473 (chicken dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for the roasted chicken component of this composite rice dish.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite code for roasted chicken drumstick rice as a plated dish; A0FJP (cooked chicken meat) is the closest defensible single-component anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of roasted chicken drumstick rice as served at a Singapore hawker centre typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising one roasted chicken drumstick (approximately 120–130 g) and a serving of chicken-flavoured rice (approximately 200–220 g) with optional garnishes and gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Roasted Chicken Drumstick Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Roasted Chicken Drumstick Rice (烧鸡腿饭), which is a canonical Singapore hawker dish entry. All per-100g values are taken from this record with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input. Saturated fat data not reported in HPB record; set to -1 with documentation in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database entry for white rice (cooked, Singaporean-style)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Roasted Chicken Drumstick Rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked white rice (jasmine/long-grain, GI ~72, high category), which constitutes the dominant carbohydrate component (~80% of total carbohydrate mass) of this dish. The chicken drumstick and seasonings contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × (25.19 g/100g × 350g serving) / 100) = 72 × 0.88165 ≈ 63.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100g, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100g; soy-based marinade and gravy are primary sodium contributors
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced gravy and remove the chicken skin to limit sodium and saturated fat intake; the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (0.71) provides partial mitigation, but a 350g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium, which represents 35–40% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base with GI ~72 and 25.19 g/100g carbohydrate creates a high glycaemic load (~63.5 GL per 350g serving) that is clinically significant for postprandial glucose management
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or reduce portion size to half a plate, remove the chicken skin to reduce fat-mediated insulin resistance, and add non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Roasting at high temperature generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) in the chicken skin; total fat 4.21 g/100g is moderate but saturated fat fraction is undocumented; sodium at 231 mg/100g adds cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should remove the chicken drumstick skin to reduce AGE and saturated fat exposure, request minimal gravy to control sodium, and consider this dish an occasional rather than daily meal given the combined sodium, fat, and refined-carbohydrate profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-temperature roasting generates AGEs that promote joint inflammation; skin-on preparation increases saturated fat load
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should remove the chicken skin to reduce AGE and saturated fat load, consider requesting steamed chicken as a lower-AGE alternative, and retain ginger-based marinade components for their anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Roasted Chicken Drumstick Rice (烧鸡腿饭) is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of a whole roasted chicken drumstick served over fragrant chicken-flavoured white rice. The chicken drumstick is marinated in a blend of soy sauce, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and ginger, then roasted at high temperature until the skin is crispy and deeply caramelised while the meat remains juicy. The rice is cooked in chicken stock and rendered chicken fat, producing a fragrant, slightly oily, and flavourful base similar to Hainanese chicken rice but with a roasted rather than poached protein. The dish is typically served with a side of cucumber slices, a small bowl of clear soup, and condiments such as chilli sauce and dark soy sauce. Nutritionally, per 100g the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5g protein, 4.21g fat, and 25.19g carbohydrate, with notable dietary fibre at 3.7g and potassium at 327mg. The dominant carbohydrate source is white rice with a GI of approximately 72 (high), making glycaemic load management important for diabetic individuals. Sodium at 231mg/100g is borderline for hypertension management but is partially offset by the high potassium content. The roasting preparation generates advanced glycation end-products in the chicken skin, which is a consideration for individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis or cardiovascular disease. This dish is a staple lunch and dinner option at Singapore hawker centres and food courts, representing Chinese-Singaporean culinary tradition with Hainanese and Cantonese roast-meat influences.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database, which is the primary approved source for Singapore hawker dishes. The input record carries confidence 0.95 with provenance marked as canonical and review_status as reviewed. The exact dish name matches the HPB database entry. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown are not reported in the HPB record, which is a known limitation of this source for composite dishes.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Roasted Chicken Drumstick Rice is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish as of 2026-04-16. USDA FoodData Central does not have an exact match for this composite Singaporean dish. 2. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo database for this dish entry — noted as potentially reflecting the composite dish average including the rice component diluting the cholesterol contribution of the chicken. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data beyond the HPB-reported fields are not available in the HPB Foodo database for this composite dish entry as of 2026-04-16. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. Glycemic load per 350g serving = 63.5. 5. Halal status is assumed based on the dish being a standard hawker centre offering; kosher status is not applicable. 6. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for this specific composite Singaporean dish.
